coredata.dc:title: Luminescence in laser-generated functionalized carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Torrisi, L.
Silipigni, L.
Torrisi, A.
Cutroneo, M.
coredata.dc:description: Biocompatible carbon dots (CDs) were obtained by the laser ablation of vegetable carbon in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution. The carbon nanoparticles were generated by an IR laser beam at a 970 nm wavelength with a spot of 200 μm diameter, pulse of 100 ms and of 350 mJ energy, at a 10 Hz repetition rate. The ablation rate, in terms of removed carbon mass per laser shot, was evaluated to be about 5.3 ng. UV–Vis spectroscopy and luminescence measurements were employed to evince the CDs luminescence. The UV excitation at a 365 nm wavelength induces CDs luminescence mainly at about 478 nm and 518 nm giving to the emitted light a characteristic blue-green color. The CDs luminescence in biocompatible solutions is presented and discussed as possible applications to the bio-medical field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nanoparticles
Luminescence
PBS
Optical spectroscopy
Laser ablation in liquids

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work has been supported by the MIUR, PRIN-PNRR Project entitled \u201CCarbon dots for advanced bio-medical imaging and beyond (CAR-BIO-MED)\u201D, CUP J53D23OL622000L - CI P2022M33MS, coordinated at MIFT Department of Messina University by Prof. L. Torrisi.

This work has been supported by the MIUR , PRIN-PNRR Project entitled \u201CCarbon dots for advanced bio-medical imaging and beyond (CAR-BIO-MED)\u201D, CUP F53D23010420001 - CI J53D23016220001, coordinated at MIFT Department of Messina University by Prof. L. Torrisi.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the vegetable carbon laser ablation apparatus (a); photos of: the carbon target (b), the experimental set-up (c) and the luminescence of C-dots induced by UV in a glass beaker (d).
Experimental set-up for optical luminescence spectroscopy (a) and luminescent C-dots in quartz cuvette under UV irradiation (b).
ATR-FTIR spectrum of the vegetable carbon target.
Comparison between the emission spectra of PBS solution without (a) and with CDs (b) when irradiated by UV light at 365 nm.
CDs luminescence yield in PBS induced by a 365 nm UV lamp versus wavelength and laser ablation time (a) and luminescence yield linearity with the laser irradiation time (b).
UV–Vis absorbance spectra of CDs in PBS at two different CDs concentrations.

head.ce:title: Luminescence in laser-generated functionalized carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dipartimento di Scienze Matematiche e Informatiche, Scienze Fisiche e Scienze della Terra, MIFT, Università di Messina, V.le F. Stagno d’Alcontres 31, 98166 Messina, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dipartimento di Scienze Matematiche e Informatiche
Scienze Fisiche e Scienze della Terra

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Dipartimento di Scienze Matematiche e Informatiche, Scienze Fisiche e Scienze della Terra, MIFT, Università di Messina, V.le F. Stagno d’Alcontres 31, 98166 Messina, Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Medicine and Surgery, Kore University of Enna, 94100 Enna, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Medicine and Surgery, Kore University of Enna, 94100 Enna, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biocompatible carbon dots (CDs) were obtained by the laser ablation of vegetable carbon in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution. The carbon nanoparticles were generated by an IR laser beam at a 970 nm wavelength with a spot of 200 μm diameter, pulse of 100 ms and of 350 mJ energy, at a 10 Hz repetition rate. The ablation rate, in terms of removed carbon mass per laser shot, was evaluated to be about 5.3 ng. UV–Vis spectroscopy and luminescence measurements were employed to evince the CDs luminescence. The UV excitation at a 365 nm wavelength induces CDs luminescence mainly at about 478 nm and 518 nm giving to the emitted light a characteristic blue-green color. The CDs luminescence in biocompatible solutions is presented and discussed as possible applications to the bio-medical field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) are more and more used to realize biocompatible prostheses, biomedical devices, electrochemical sensors, drugs and tissue carriers and biological fluid markers [1–5]. CNPs can be prepared by different techniques using top-down methods, such as carbon-based chemically reactive elements [6], or bottom-up methods, such as the laser ablation of carbon-based materials in liquids [7]. Also, such processes may use ultrasonic treatments, microwaves, thermal treatments, plasma etching and others.

Usually, source materials are represented by graphite, amorphous carbon, graphene oxide, natural carbon, and elements rich in carbon such as honey [8], cane sugar [9], orange juice [10], and grape juice [11].

Water-soluble carbon nanoparticles (W-CNPs) are reported in literature as result of laser ablation of carbon targets placed in liquids [12–14]. In fact, the use of pulsed lasers irradiating solid targets placed in liquids produces an ionized vapour (plasma) at the solid–liquid interface which, expanding in the liquid, induces atomic nucleation inside the gas bubble. When the bubble becomes too expanded and its pressure reduces, it collapses, releasing nanoparticles into the liquid [15,16]. This process produces nanoparticles which generally have a spherical shape with a size, which increases with the laser pulse intensity, i.e. with the laser pulse energy, and reduces with the spot size and laser pulse duration [7,17].

The pulsed laser ablation (PLA) in organic liquids represents a useful and highly efficient technique for the luminescent and biocompatible nano-sized C-dots (CDs) generation, as reported in the literature [18,19]. The so obtained C-dots possess high luminescence, generally induced by UV or near UV light irradiation, which results very interesting for bioimaging applications [20]. Studies of HeLa, MCF-7 and PanC-1 cells through C-dots luminescence are in progress [22,23].

The novelty of this study consists in the demonstration that a simple diode laser operating in the IR region with long temporal pulses (100 ms) can be capable of producing C-dots from charcoal placed in a biological liquid, creating green and biocompatible dispersion with high luminescence. In fact, literature does not report effects of such laser in charcoal placed in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution, such as investigated in this experiment.

A diode Laser (Sirolaser) operating at the 970 nm wavelength and at a 10 Hz repetition rate with a pulse duration of 100 ms and a pulse energy of 350 mJ, was employed to perform the carbon ablation process in liquids [24]. Laser operates in the air, with 200 μm spot diameter (optical fiber output diameter), an intensity of 1.11 × 104 W/cm2 and a fluence of 1.11 kJ/cm2.

Vegetable carbon (Charcoal) 100 % was used as target. Charcoal is a fuel produced by the carbonization process of wood, which consists in the transformation of an organic compound into coal. It is a process that occurs during the heating of wood (fuel) to high temperature in the presence of little oxygen (oxidant). Although its composition can change with the type of charred vegetable, with the geographical location of sampling and with the used charring process (temperature, quantity of oxygen, pressure), the following wt % concentrations average composition, with large variations, can be achieved according to the literature [25]: C = 66.9 %: H = 4.4 %; O = 7.6 %; N = 1.3 %; S = 1.1 %; Moisture = 7.2 %; Ash = 11.5 %; Cl = 0.1 %. Many functional groups are present in this type of carbon, such as hydroxyl (–OH), epoxide (−C-O-C-), carbonyl (C = O), carboxyl (O = C-OH), H2O, CO2, and others. Some principal physical properties of this carbon consist in its amorphous nature, low density, high porosity, high absorbance in the visible region and black colour, 10–50 µm particles size, pH = 7–8, low thermal and electrical conductivity, low mechanical resistance, and elasticity.

The laser ablation was conducted in distilled water and in 10X phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution by Sigma-Aldrich. This is a 10-fold concentrated blend of phosphate buffer and saline solution with a pH = 7.4 and RNase-free, typically used in molecular Biology [26]. The laser irradiation was performed in air, at room temperature (24 °C) and 1 atm pressure, irradiating a vegetable carbon target with a near parallelepiped shape, 6.3 cm2 surface, 5 mm thickness and a pristine mass of about 1.98 g. The C target is placed in a glass beaker under 1 mm surface liquid and the laser light irradiates through 200 μm optical fiber placed at 1 mm distance from the liquid surface, as schematized in Fig. 1 a. Due to the low target density, it tends to float in the liquid. To drain the carbon nanoparticles produced by the laser ablation towards the liquid of the beaker, the target was first completely immersed in the liquid for a few minutes and subsequently made to float so that a layer formed on its surface of liquid with a thickness of around 1 mm.

The beaker is placed on a slow rotating disk to generate laser ablation in different areas of the carbon target. Fig. 1b shows a photo of the used carbon target. Fig. 1c shows the entire experimental apparatus used for the laser ablation in liquid. Fig. 1d shows a photo of the luminescent liquid in the beaker containing CDs under UV irradiation at the 365 nm wavelength. The luminescence has a characteristic green–blue color.

The JASCO 4600 Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer equipped with an ATR (Attenuated Total Reflectance) accessory was employed in the (400–4000) cm−1 wavenumber region with a minimal spectral resolution of about 0.7 cm− 1.
The JASCO double beam V-750 UV/Vis/NIR spectrophotometer was used to perform absorbance measurements of the prepared dispersion placed in quartz cuvette, in the (250–700) nm wavelength range.

The Avantes AvaSpec-2048-USB2 fiber optic spectrometer was employed for measurements of luminescence of the in solution prepared C-dots performed illuminating the prepared dispersion with an UV lamp (Blak-Ray lamp, model UVL-56) emitting around a 365 nm wavelength. The UV lamp source fluence, measured in our laboratory at a distance of 10 cm using a laser power meter (Nova Display 7Z01500) connected to a highly sensitive calibrated laser calorimeter head, was of about 100 mJ/cm2.
The luminescence induced by UV lamp was monitored using an optical fiber (300 μm diameter), connected to the spectrometer from the UV region (300 nm) up to the visible region (700 nm).

Optical microscopy was used to observe the craters produced by the laser ablation in the targets. Fig. 2 a shows the set-up employed for the measurements of UV induced luminescence. Fig. 2b reports a photo of CDs in the PBS solution placed in a quartz cuvette under UV (at a 365 nm wavelength) irradiation.

The used target of vegetable carbon was submitted to mass density measures and ATR-FTIR analysis for a further characterization. The first measures give an average density ρ = 0.63 g/cm3 with a variation of about 5 %, in agreement with the literature [25]. The second measure shows different oxygen functional groups present in the used target, as one can see in the spectrum of Fig. 3 . Here are evident the large and intense hydroxyl (–OH) peak, the carboxylic (C = O), the intense C = C double bond peak, the carbon nitride (C–N) and other groups. The average IR transmittance in this region is high and of about 98 %. The spectrum agrees with the literature data [27].

At 3650 cm−1 wavenumber typically can be observed the following functional groups: hydroxyl stretching of free OH groups, such as those in alcohols and phenols, which can be originated from the use of PBS solution [28]; Si–OH groups present as contaminants in the analyzed charcoal sample [29].

The laser ablation of vegetable carbon in distilled water, using the same described parameters of laser wavelength, energy and fluence, has produced carbon nanoparticles and CDs but they are not luminescent under excitation by UV light. This result was also obtained using many lasers operating at different wavelengths (1064 nm, 532 nm and 632.8 nm), different pulse durations (100 ms, 10 ms, 3 ns) and energies (350 mJ, 100 mJ, 50 mJ), different fluences and intensities and exciting the obtained dispersions with UV, deuterium and halogen lamps.

Actually, we don’t know the reason for the not luminescence of CDs produced in distilled water. May be that the luminescence of CDs produced in PBS is induced by the nanoparticles functionalization due to phosporus, sodium, clorine, or different functionalized carbon and oxygen groups presents in the dispersion. Such molecules may cover the carbon nanoparticles and facilitate electronic excitation and de-excitation transitions giving rise to characteristic luminescence, in accordance with the literature [30].

The C-dot dispersion excitation has been investigated using the UV lamp centered at 365 nm wavelength. Generally, the laser irradiation has been performed at a 10 Hz repetition rate and the irradiation time ranged between 20 min and 200 min in a slow rotating (50 rpm) beaker with the laser fiber placed orthogonally to the liquid surface and to the target surface, as in Fig. 1a.

The laser ablation of vegetable carbon produces a plasma at the solid–liquid interface with the formation of an ionized gas bubble which expands in the liquid up to collapse with release of the nucleated C atoms forming CDs. Of course, also micrometric particles are produced by the ablation, and generally they are called CNPs, which fall to the bottom of the beaker when the dispersion is left at rest for a long time. As will be said later, the expected dimensions for the CDs produced are between more than 2 nm and less than 10 nm. The CNPs produced have dimensions from 10 nm up.

The ablation rate was evaluated measuring the mass of removed carbon from the pristine target. In particular, Fig. 1b shows two craters produced by the laser operating on a fix target for long irradiation times (200 min). The removed volume of vegetable carbon in a 200 min irradiation time at 10 Hz corresponds to about 1 mm3, i.e. to a mass of about 0.63 mg. Thus, the ablation rate Y of the target in a first approximation is of about:
(1)
Y = 0.63 m g / 1.2 x
10 5
s h o t s = 5 .
2510
- 6
m g / s h o t = 5.25 n g / s h o t

Generally, the removed mass of the ablated target was released to a PBS volume of 20 ml, producing, in a 200 min irradiation time, a PBS solution with carbon atoms with a concentration of about 31.5 μg/ml. Of course, not all ablated carbon atoms produce CDs, probably only the 10 % of the ablated mass generates CDs, as evaluated approximately measuring the precipitate residual carbon mass in the dispersion. Thus, assuming the CDs present in the dispersion to be about 3.1 μg/ml, the luminescence induced by UV irradiation is strongly evident, as reported in the photo of Fig. 2b. The dispersion appears blue-green colored under UV irradiation and is practically transparent in the visible light.

Fig. 4 shows a comparison between the emission spectra obtained irradiating with the UV lamp a quartz cuvette containing the PBS solution without the CDs (to have information on the background signal) (a), and with CDs (b). The pure PBS solution does not show luminescence by UV, and the spectrum indicates only a low background signal in the (400 – 650) nm region under irradiation of the UV lamp peak centered at 365 nm and large 34 nm (full-width at half-maximum, FWHM) (Fig. 4a). Instead, the PBS solution with CDs shows luminescence, as one can see in Fig. 4b, using a 200 min laser irradiation time at which the CDs concentration should be about 3.1 μg/ml, as above described. The same CDs concentration in water does not produce any luminescence. Two peaks are present in the emission spectrum of the PBS solution with CDs: the first centered at about 478 nm (blue color) with a width at half maximum of about 35 nm (FWHM), and the second peak centered at about 518 nm (green color) with a width at half maximum of about 25 nm (FWHM).

The CDs luminescence peaks induced by UV are well reproducible and grow about linearly with the CDs concentration, i.e. with the laser irradiation time, as evident in the emission spectra comparison of Fig. 5 a and in Fig. 5b in which the laser irradiation time dependence of luminescence yield (signal minus background) is shown. It is possible to constate a very good proportionality between the laser irradiation time and the CDs luminescence yield in the (400–550) nm wavelength region, i.e. with the CDs concentration in PBS solution between 0.93 μg/ml and 3.1 μg/ml. The luminescence measurements are affected by an error of about 6 %.

Fig. 6 shows a comparison in the (250–700) nm region between the UV–Vis extinction (absorbance) spectra of two samples of CDs in PBS obtained from the vegetable carbon target at a laser irradiation time of 100 min (black curve) and 200 min (red curve), i.e. at the two CDs concentrations in PBS of about 1.55 μg/ml and 3.1 μg/ml respectively.

The data comparison shows that the increase in the laser irradiation time induces an increment of the dispersion CDs concentration causing an expected enhancement of the absorbance due to a higher density of CDs (desired) and CNPs (unwanted) in the PBS solution with consequent minor optical transparency of the dispersion.

The extinction trend, illustrated in Fig. 6 and characterized by a strong optical absorption in the UV region with a long tail extending into the visible range, is typical of fluorescent CDs as reported in the literature [31]. In particular, CDs display a short wavelength band below 300 nm, visible if not obscured by the strong absorption background, corresponding to a π → π* transitions involving the aromatic sp2 carbons (the aromatic C = C bonds), and a tail starting from 300 nm to 400 nm assigned to the intrinsic absorption of n → π* transition of the C = O bond in carbon cores, where n indicates the n states, i.e. the nonbonding state in oxygen atoms. Other absorption peaks at λ > 400 nm can be attributed to the surface states containing electron-hole pairs and can overlap with the n–π* transition [32,33]. On the basis of this information, the two shoulders visible at about 355 nm and 332 nm in the absorbance spectra of Fig. 6 can be assigned to n → π* transitions of C = O or other bonds on the CDs surface. It is worth noting that the energy difference between the two structures (at 478 nm and at 518 nm) in the emission spectrum is about 0.2 eV, which is equal to the energy difference in the two UV absorption features (at 355 nm and at 332 nm). This seems indicates that the blue-green luminescence exhibited by C-dots (see Fig. 2b) at the wavelengths of about 478 nm and 518 nm can be associated to π* → n transitions of electrons, in agreement with the literature [31,33].

Since CDs with a larger diameter (∼10 nm) will usually emit a larger wavelength, with colours such as orange or red, while CDs with a smaller size (∼2 nm) emit shorter wavelengths, with colours such as violet or blue [34], the blue-green luminescence that we have detected is expected for CDs with size less than 10 nm but greater than 2 nm, a size which will be investigate using transmission electron microscopy and discussed in a next paper.

The novelty of this research work consists in producing CDs with simple and low-cost equipment. It is twofold: on the one hand the use of a reliable IR diode laser with low energy and long pulses, very widespread in laboratories all over the world; on the other, the ablation of a simple, biocompatible and common vegetal carbon target that allows the generation of small nanoparticles in PBS solutions. The technique presented allows the generation of CDs dispersed in a green and perfectly biocompatible solution which, without further treatments, can be used for various applications including optical sensing and bioimaging.

The vegetable carbon has peculiar properties in medicine and biology, such as its strong absorbent properties towards specific toxins and poisons, that make it idoneous for different. For example, vegetable charcoal has the ability to retain on its surface liquids, gases, bacteria, pathogens, toxins and viruses present in the gastrointestinal tract. CDs represent a class of carbon-based nanostructures with great potential in the field of targeted chemotherapy. CDs can be employed for bioimaging, sensing, therapy, having low cytotoxicity, high water solubility, high photostability, and tunable excitation wavelength-dependent emission properties [35].

Blue-green coloured CDs luminescence has been obtained by the CDs synthesis using laser ablation of vegetable carbon in a PBS solution at room temperature. Only about the 10 % of the laser ablated material forms CDs. Dispersions with a concentration of about 3.1 μg/ml of CDs in PBS show a significant luminescence in the (400–650) nm region under UV irradiation at 365 nm. The same CDs concentration in water, instead, does not produce any luminescence.
Luminescence was investigated with a sensitive spectrophotometer indicating a good proportionality of the quantum yield with the laser ablation time, i.e. with the dispersion concentration.

UV–Vis spectroscopy has evinced n–π* transitions probably responsible of the blue-green observed luminescence that in turn seems to suggest the formation of CDs with size less than 10 nm but greater than 2 nm.

This investigation is still ongoing because the mechanisms that give rise to luminescence are not yet well known. However, it is thought that compounds present in PBS, such as phosphorus, sodium, chlorine and other carbon and oxygen functional groups, can bind to the surfaces of small carbon aggregates, coating them and giving them surface electronic transition states of the valence band that, following UV excitation, upon de-excitation can give rise to emission in the visible band of the blue-green area.
Further investigations are in progress to measure the synthetized CDs size and to evince the possibility to use such biocompatible luminescent dispersions in biological field and in optical sensing.

L. Torrisi: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition. L. Silipigni: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. A. Torrisi: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition. M. Cutroneo: Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been supported by the MIUR, PRIN-PNRR Project entitled “Carbon dots for advanced bio-medical imaging and beyond (CAR-BIO-MED)”, CUP J53D23OL622000L - CI P2022M33MS, coordinated at MIFT Department of Messina University by Prof. L. Torrisi.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Formulation, pretreatment, and densification options to improve biomass specifications for co-firing high percentages with coal
